volunteer_background.how_did_you_hear_about_the_family_effect:
I first heard about The Family Effect through a combination of my workplace and a local community event. I work as a school counselor at Greenville Middle Academy, and last spring our principal forwarded an email from a local nonprofit networking group that highlighted organizations supporting families affected by substance use. The Family Effect was featured in that email with a short description of their programs and a link to their website.

A few weeks later, I attended a community resource fair hosted at a nearby church. The Family Effect had a table there with information about their programs, especially the ones focused on supporting children while their parents receive treatment. I spoke for about 20 minutes with a staff member named Laura, who explained how volunteers help with homework support, family nights, and childcare during group sessions. That conversation stuck with me.

After that, I went home and spent a good amount of time browsing The Family Effect website, reading client stories and the annual report. I also recognized the name from a former student’s parent, who once mentioned getting help from a “family-focused program connected to treatment services” when we were talking about resources. When I put all of that together, I realized The Family Effect was exactly the kind of organization I had been looking for.

So, in short: I heard about The Family Effect through a school email, a community resource fair, and then did my own online research, which really confirmed my interest in getting involved.
volunteer_background.why_do_you_want_to_volunteer_with_us:
I want to volunteer with The Family Effect because your mission sits right at the intersection of the three things I care most about: children, families, and recovery. In my day job as a middle school counselor, I see firsthand how substance use and untreated mental health challenges in a family ripple out to affect children’s behavior, attendance, and self-esteem. Often, the kids I work with are carrying a lot of responsibility and worry that they’re too young to even put into words.

Over the last few years, I’ve had several students quietly share that a parent or close relative is in treatment or struggling with addiction. When those families are connected with supportive services, like counseling and family programs, the difference in the child’s outlook is dramatic. They become more hopeful, less guarded, and more able to focus on being kids again. I’ve had this growing feeling that I want to be involved not just at the school level, but on the family and community side as well.

From what I’ve learned, The Family Effect is very intentional about treating the family as a whole, not just the individual in treatment. I really appreciate that approach. I know from my own extended family’s experience with addiction that when one person is in crisis, everyone around them is affected—and when one person starts to heal, everyone needs support to adjust and grow.

I’m also drawn to the practical, hands-on nature of the volunteer roles you describe. I’m not just looking to show up once for a photo-op; I want a consistent, relationship-based way to contribute. Helping kids with homework while their parents attend a group, supporting family nights, or just being a calm, encouraging adult presence feels very aligned with what I already do and enjoy.

On a more personal note, I had an uncle who struggled with alcohol use for most of my childhood. He eventually got into a recovery program when I was in high school, but I remember how isolated my cousins felt before that. There weren’t many spaces where they could talk openly about what was going on at home, and they carried a lot of shame that really wasn’t theirs to carry. Watching them go through that has stayed with me, and it’s a big part of why I care so much about family-centered support.

Volunteering with The Family Effect feels like a meaningful way to combine my professional skills, my personal values, and my desire to be part of a long-term solution. I don’t expect to “fix” anything on my own, but I do believe that showing up reliably, listening without judgment, and offering concrete help can make a real difference for kids and parents who are doing the hard work of rebuilding their lives.
volunteer_background.what_special_skills_and_or_qualification:
I bring a mix of professional training, practical experience with children and families, and strong organizational and communication skills that I believe would be useful to The Family Effect.

1. Professional background with youth and families:
- I have a Master’s degree in School Counseling from Clemson University (graduated 2017).
- For the past 7 years, I’ve worked as a full-time school counselor at Greenville Middle Academy.
- In that role, I provide individual and small-group counseling for students ages 11–14 on issues like anxiety, grief, family conflict, and academic stress.
- I regularly collaborate with parents, teachers, and outside therapists to support students, which has given me a lot of experience navigating sensitive family situations with respect and confidentiality.

2. Experience with trauma-informed and strengths-based approaches:
- I’ve completed professional development workshops on trauma-informed care, motivational interviewing basics, and de-escalation strategies for youth.
- I’m comfortable working with children who may be guarded, anxious, or acting out because of what’s happening at home.
- I try to approach every child and parent from a strengths-based perspective—focusing on what they’re doing well and what’s possible, rather than just what’s going wrong.

3. Practical skills working directly with children:
- I run weekly lunch groups at school (on topics like coping skills, friendship, and self-esteem), so I’m used to facilitating small groups, keeping kids engaged, and setting gentle but clear boundaries.
- I regularly help students with homework organization and study skills, which would translate well to homework support roles.
- I’m patient and calm, even when kids are having a rough day. I’m used to helping them name their feelings and find appropriate ways to express them.

4. Communication and relationship-building:
- I’m comfortable talking with parents from many different backgrounds and levels of comfort with schools and social services.
- I make a point of being non-judgmental and practical, especially when families are overwhelmed or embarrassed about their situation.
- I’m bilingual in English and conversational Spanish (I’m not fluent enough for therapy-level conversations, but I can greet families, give simple instructions, and help them feel more welcome).

5. Organizational and reliability skills:
- As the only counselor for my grade level, I manage a busy schedule, track student progress, and keep detailed (and confidential) notes.
- I’m very reliable with time commitments—if I sign up to be somewhere, I treat it as seriously as a work obligation.
- I’m comfortable following agency procedures, including confidentiality policies, mandated reporting rules, and safety protocols.

6. Prior volunteer and community experience:
- I’ve volunteered for three years with a local after-school reading program, where I helped elementary students who were a grade level or more behind in reading. That taught me a lot about patience and celebrating small wins.
- I’ve helped coordinate a “family resource night” at my school for the past two years, working with community partners to share information about food assistance, counseling, and other services.

Overall, I think the combination of my counseling background, my comfort working with kids in challenging circumstances, and my steady, low-drama personality would make me a dependable and supportive volunteer for your programs.
volunteer_background.what_are_your_hobbies_and_interests:
Outside of work, I have a fairly full life with a mix of hobbies that help me stay grounded and connected to my community.

1. Reading and lifelong learning:
- I’m an avid reader, especially of contemporary fiction and memoirs that focus on families, resilience, and social issues. Recently I’ve read “Beautiful Boy” by David Sheff and “The Glass Castle” by Jeannette Walls, both of which deepened my empathy for families navigating addiction and instability.
- I also like to read practical books on psychology and education. I’m the kind of person who actually enjoys attending workshops and taking notes.

2. Walking, hiking, and being outdoors:
- I walk in my neighborhood most evenings after dinner, both to clear my head and to stay active.
- On weekends, I like to go for longer hikes with my partner and our dog (a very energetic mixed-breed rescue named Milo). We often go to Paris Mountain State Park or along the Swamp Rabbit Trail.

3. Creative and relaxing hobbies:
- I enjoy basic watercolor painting—not anything fancy, but enough to relax and feel creative. I mostly paint landscapes and simple plants.
- I keep a small balcony container garden with herbs (basil, mint, rosemary) and a few seasonal flowers. Tending to plants has become a quiet, grounding routine for me.

4. Community and relationships:
- I host a monthly potluck dinner with a small group of friends and neighbors. We rotate apartments and each bring a dish; it’s become a little community ritual where we check in on each other’s lives.
- I’m involved in a local book club that meets every other month at a coffee shop downtown. We read a mix of fiction and non-fiction and often end up discussing larger themes like mental health, inequality, and family dynamics.

5. Mindfulness and self-care:
- I practice gentle yoga at home using online videos a few times a week, mostly for stress management.
- I keep a simple journal where I reflect on my day and note three things I’m grateful for; this started as a coping tool during the early days of the pandemic and has stuck.

Overall, my hobbies reflect a need for balance: I spend a lot of emotional energy at work, so I gravitate toward activities that are calming, creative, and relationship-oriented. I think this balance helps me show up more fully when I’m supporting kids and families, whether at school or as a volunteer.
volunteer_background.what_was_your_most_memorable_volunteer_e:
My most memorable volunteer experience was with an after-school reading program at a community center on the west side of town, where I tutored a third-grade student named “J.” (I’ll keep his name initial for privacy). I worked with him twice a week for an entire school year, and that experience really shaped how I think about consistency and the long-term impact of showing up.

When I first met J., he was very quiet and avoided eye contact. His reading level was about a year and a half behind, and he would often say things like “I’m just not good at this” or “I’m dumb.” In our first few sessions, he would shut down if he made a mistake, sometimes putting his head on the table and refusing to keep going. I later learned from the program coordinator that there was a lot of instability at home, including a relative who was struggling with substance use and frequently in and out of the household.

At the beginning, I made a conscious decision that my main goals for him would be: 1) to help him feel safe and respected during our sessions, and 2) to help him experience success in small, very concrete ways. We developed a little routine: he would pick out one “fun” book (usually something about animals or superheroes) and I would bring a short, leveled reading passage that was just slightly above his comfort zone. We used a lot of praise focused on effort—things like, “I noticed you sounded that out even though it was tricky” or “You stuck with that whole page without giving up.”

There was one particular day about three months in that stands out. J. had had a tough day at school and came into the tutoring room looking angry and withdrawn. He told me he didn’t want to read and that he “hated books.” Instead of pushing the reading right away, I asked if he would be willing to tell me about his day while we drew on scrap paper. As he drew, he gradually opened up about an argument at home the night before and how he hadn’t slept well. I mostly just listened and reflected back what I heard: that he was tired, frustrated, and feeling like everything was his fault.

After about 15 minutes, I asked if he’d be willing to read just one page of a silly animal book we had used before. He agreed, on the condition that he could pick which page. He ended up reading three pages. At the end of the session, he said, almost offhandedly, “This wasn’t that bad.” It was a small comment, but it felt huge.

By the end of the school year, his reading assessment showed that he had improved by almost a full grade level. But what meant even more to me was the change in his attitude. He started greeting me with, “What are we reading today?” instead of, “Do we have to?” He began to see himself as someone who could learn and improve, not just as “the kid who’s behind.”

That experience was memorable because it underscored that academic progress and emotional support are deeply intertwined. J. didn’t just need phonics practice; he needed a calm, predictable adult who believed he could grow and who wasn’t going to disappear after a few weeks. It also taught me that sometimes the most important thing you can do as a volunteer is to adjust your plan in the moment—like drawing for a bit before reading—so that the child feels seen and regulated.

I still think about J. when I consider new volunteer roles. It reminds me that even a few hours a week, when offered consistently and with genuine care, can make a real difference in how a child sees themselves and their future. That’s the kind of impact I hope to have with families and children through The Family Effect as well.
